1: κυριεύει τοῦ ἀνθρώπου (kyrieuei tou anthrōpou) — "lords it over a person" — kyrieuō (exercise lordship over) with the genitive: the law's dominion is the legal claim it has over the living. The principle Paul will illustrate with the marriage analogy: death terminates legal obligation.
2: τῷ ζῶντι ἀνδρὶ δέδεται νόμῳ (tō zōnti andri dedetai nomō) — "she is bound by law to the living husband" — dedetai (perfect passive: has been bound and remains bound) — marriage as a permanent legal obligation while both parties live. ἐὰν δὲ ἀποθάνῃ ὁ ἀνήρ — "but if the husband dies" — death is the one event that dissolves the legal bond; Paul's analogy requires that the believer die (with Christ) to be freed from the law's claim.
3: ἐλευθέρα ἐστὶν ἀπὸ τοῦ νόμου (eleuthera estin apo tou nomou) — "she is free from the law" — the marriage analogy establishes the principle: death releases from legal obligation. The wife who remarries while her husband lives is a moichalis (adulteress); but if he has died, the same action is lawful. Paul will apply this: the believer died with Christ and is therefore free to "marry" (be united with) the risen Christ without violating the law.
4: ὥστε, ἀδελφοί μου, καὶ ὑμεῖς ἐθανατώθητε τῷ νόμῳ διὰ τοῦ σώματος τοῦ Χριστοῦ — "so then, my brothers and sisters, you also were put to death with regard to the law through the body of Christ." Note the asymmetry with the analogy: in the analogy the husband dies; in the application the wife (believer) dies. Paul's application inverts the subject of death because believers are united with Christ who died; they die through his body, not he through theirs. εἰς τὸ γενέσθαι ὑμᾶς ἑτέρῳ — "so that you might belong to another" — the purpose clause: death-with-Christ enables new belonging to the risen Christ; the new husband is the resurrection life.
5: ἐν τῇ σαρκί (en tē sarki) — "in the flesh" — not the physical body per se but the mode of existence characterized by the Adamic nature. The flesh-era is the pre-Christ condition; the Spirit-era is the new-creation condition. τὰ παθήματα τῶν ἁμαρτιῶν τὰ διὰ τοῦ νόμου — "the passions of sins through the law" — the law's role in arousing the very sins it prohibits is Paul's theme; the dia tou nomou (through the law) is the disturbing claim that the holy law is the mechanism through which sinful passions operate.
6: κατηργήθημεν ἀπὸ τοῦ νόμου (katērgēthēmen apo tou nomou) — "we have been released from the law" — katargeō again: the law has been rendered inoperative in its claim on those who have died with Christ. ἐν καινότητι πνεύματος καὶ οὐ παλαιότητι γράμματος (en kainotēti pneumatos kai ou palaiōtēti grammatos) — "in newness of Spirit and not in oldness of letter." The pneuma/gramma antithesis (Spirit/letter) is the Pauline expression for the new-covenant/old-covenant distinction; the letter kills (because it condemns without empowering), the Spirit gives life (2 Corinthians 3:6).
7: τί οὖν ἐροῦμεν; ὁ νόμος ἁμαρτία (ti oun eroumen; ho nomos hamartia) — "what then shall we say? Is the law sin?" The implication of v5-6 (sin works through the law): is the law itself sinful? Paul's mē genoito is emphatic. τὴν ἁμαρτίαν οὐκ ἔγνων εἰ μὴ διὰ νόμου — "I would not have known sin except through the law" — the law's diagnostic function: it reveals what sin is by naming it. τὴν ἐπιθυμίαν οὐκ ᾔδειν — "I would not have known covetousness" — the last commandment of the Decalogue (οὐκ ἐπιθυμήσεις) is the example Paul chooses — perhaps because it addresses the interior desire rather than the external act, making it the commandment that most clearly exposes the depth of sin.
8: ἀφορμὴν δὲ λαβοῦσα ἡ ἁμαρτία (aphormēn de labousa hē hamartia) — "but sin, seizing an opportunity" — aphormē (base of operations, starting point for a military expedition); sin uses the commandment as its beachhead. κατειργάσατο ἐν ἐμοὶ πᾶσαν ἐπιθυμίαν — "produced in me all kinds of covetousness" — the commandment's naming of the forbidden desire becomes the stimulus that sin exploits to produce more of the very thing forbidden.
9: ἐγὼ δὲ ἔζων χωρὶς νόμου ποτέ (egō de ezōn chōris nomou pote) — "I was once alive apart from the law" — the autobiographical/representative egō describes a pre-commandment state of living. This may be: (a) Paul's own pre-Torah-consciousness childhood; (b) Adam before the commandment; (c) generic Israel before Sinai. All three levels are probably operative. ἐλθούσης δὲ τῆς ἐντολῆς ἡ ἁμαρτία ἀνέζησεν — "but when the commandment came, sin came to life" — aorist: sin's revival is an event triggered by the commandment's arrival.
10: καὶ εὑρέθη μοι ἡ ἐντολὴ ἡ εἰς ζωήν, αὕτη εἰς θάνατον — "and the commandment that was intended for life proved to be death for me." The divine intention of the law (eis zōēn = for life, referencing Leviticus 18:5) and its actual effect in the sin-condition (eis thanaton = for death) are both real; the law is not at fault but sin exploits it. The irony: what was given to produce life produces death because sin perverts it.
11: ἡ γὰρ ἁμαρτία ἀφορμὴν λαβοῦσα διὰ τῆς ἐντολῆς ἐξηπάτησέν με (hē gar hamartia aphormēn labousa dia tēs entolēs exēpatēsen me) — "for sin, having seized an opportunity through the commandment, deceived me." Exapataō (to thoroughly deceive) — the Eve-parallel is explicit: as the serpent used the commandment (Genesis 3:1-5) to deceive Eve, so sin uses the commandment to deceive the egō. The Genesis allusion is embedded in the verb.
12: ὥστε ὁ μὲν νόμος ἅγιος καὶ ἡ ἐντολὴ ἁγία καὶ δικαία καὶ ἀγαθή — "so then the law is holy, and the commandment is holy and just and good." Three predicates clarifying the law's character after the argument that sin exploited it: hagios (consecrated, belonging to God's sphere), dikaios (right by God's standard), agathos (genuinely good in its nature). The law's perversion by sin does not impugn the law; the fault is sin's alone.
13: ἀλλὰ ἡ ἁμαρτία, ἵνα φανῇ ἁμαρτία (alla hē hamartia, hina phanē hamartia) — "but sin, so that it might appear as sin." The purpose clause is divine: God allowed sin to work through the good commandment precisely so that sin would be fully exposed as sin — its true character revealed by its exploitation of holiness. ἵνα γένηται καθ᾽ ὑπερβολὴν ἁμαρτωλὸς ἡ ἁμαρτία — "so that sin might become utterly sinful through the commandment" — kath' hyperbolēn (beyond measure, to the extreme) — sin becomes maximally sinful when it uses the holy commandment as its instrument.
14: ὁ νόμος πνευματικός ἐστιν, ἐγὼ δὲ σάρκινός εἰμι, πεπραμένος ὑπὸ τὴν ἁμαρτίαν (ho nomos pneumatikos estin, egō de sarkinos eimi, pepramenos hypo tēn hamartian) — "the law is spiritual, but I am fleshly, sold under sin." The contrast pneumatikos/sarkinos (spiritual/fleshly) — the law belongs to God's sphere; the egō belongs to the flesh-sphere. Pepramenos (perfect passive participle: sold and remaining sold) — the slave who has been sold and cannot buy himself back. The famous identity of the egō (Christian? pre-Christian? rhetorical?) is unresolved; the present tense "I am" and "I want" in vv15-24 are the strongest evidence that Paul is describing a Christian condition under the ongoing pull of the Adamic nature.
15: ὃ γὰρ κατεργάζομαι οὐ γινώσκω (ho gar katergazomai ou ginōskō) — "for I do not understand what I am doing" — not intellectual ignorance but the breakdown between intention and action that defines the divided self. οὐ γὰρ ὃ θέλω τοῦτο πράσσω, ἀλλ᾽ ὃ μισῶ τοῦτο ποιῶ — "for what I want, this I do not practice; but what I hate, this I do" — the two present-tense verbs of action (prassō, poiō) describe habitual patterns; the thelō (want/will) describes the consistent moral intention that nevertheless fails to translate into action.
16: εἰ δὲ ὃ οὐ θέλω τοῦτο ποιῶ, σύμφημι τῷ νόμῳ ὅτι καλός — "but if I do what I do not want, I agree with the law that it is good." The egō's will (thelō) aligns with the law's standard even while actions fail to follow. This alignment of willing-with-law is itself evidence of the law's goodness and of the egō's genuine moral aspiration, however frustrated.
17: νυνὶ δὲ οὐκέτι ἐγὼ κατεργάζομαι αὐτό (nyni de ouketi egō katergazomai auto) — "but now it is no longer I who am doing it." The nyni de introduces a crucial distinction: the egō (the true, willing, law-agreeing self) and ἡ οἰκοῦσα ἐν ἐμοὶ ἁμαρτία (hē oikousa en emoi hamartia) — "sin dwelling in me" — oikeō (to dwell, inhabit) as a present participle: sin occupies the egō as an invasive resident. The distinction between the egō and the indwelling sin anticipates the body-resurrection solution.
18: οἶδα γὰρ ὅτι οὐκ οἰκεῖ ἐν ἐμοί, τοῦτ᾽ ἔστιν ἐν τῇ σαρκί μου, ἀγαθόν — "for I know that good does not dwell in me, that is, in my flesh." The qualification "that is, in my flesh" is crucial: the egō is not wholly bad; it is the sarx (flesh, the unredeemed bodily existence) in which good does not dwell. τὸ γὰρ θέλειν παράκειταί μοι, τὸ δὲ κατεργάζεσθαι τὸ καλὸν οὔ — "for wanting is present with me, but the doing of good is not" — the vocabulary of presence (parakeimai, to lie beside) contrasts the present-wanting with the absent-doing.
19: οὐ γὰρ ὃ θέλω ποιῶ ἀγαθόν, ἀλλ᾽ ὃ οὐ θέλω κακὸν τοῦτο πράσσω — "for the good that I want, I do not do; but the evil I do not want, this I practice." The exact repetition of vv15-16 with the nouns agathon (good) and kakon (evil) specified. The rhetorical repetition is Paul's technique of drilling home the severity of the divided self before the resolution of Romans 8.
20: εἰ δὲ ὃ οὐ θέλω ἐγὼ τοῦτο ποιῶ, οὐκέτι ἐγὼ κατεργάζομαι αὐτό — the same logic as v17 restated: if the egō's will is not the source of the unwanted action, the agent is the indwelling sin. The double ouketi egō (no longer I) in vv17 and 20 emphasizes the dissociation; yet this is not an evasion of responsibility but an analysis of the divided anthropology that requires the Spirit's liberation (Romans 8) for resolution.
21: εὑρίσκω ἄρα τὸν νόμον (heuriskō ara ton nomon) — "so I find the law/principle" — nomos here probably means "principle" or "pattern" rather than the Mosaic Torah; the egō discovers a law-like inevitability in its condition: when it wants to do good, evil is present alongside. The discovery is experiential, not theoretical.
22: συνήδομαι γὰρ τῷ νόμῳ τοῦ θεοῦ κατὰ τὸν ἔσω ἄνθρωπον (synēdomai gar tō nomō tou theou kata ton esō anthrōpon) — "for I delight in the law of God according to the inner person." Synēdomai (to rejoice together with, delight in) — a rare verb; the egō's delight in the law of God is genuine and deep. Esō anthrōpos (inner person) — the real self that the Spirit is renewing (2 Corinthians 4:16; Ephesians 3:16), in contrast to the flesh that resists the law.
23: βλέπω δὲ ἕτερον νόμον ἐν τοῖς μέλεσίν μου ἀντιστρατευόμενον τῷ νόμῳ τοῦ νοός μου — "but I see another law in my members warring against the law of my mind." Antistrateuomai (to wage war against, counter-campaign) — the military metaphor for the conflict within the self: two laws, two armies, the same person as the battleground. αἰχμαλωτίζοντά με τῷ νόμῳ τῆς ἁμαρτίας — "taking me captive to the law of sin" — aichmalōtizō (to take as a prisoner of war); the divided self is the prisoner of the flesh-law even while the mind agrees with God's law.
24: ταλαίπωρος ἐγὼ ἄνθρωπος· τίς με ῥύσεται ἐκ τοῦ σώματος τοῦ θανάτου τούτου (talaipōros egō anthrōpos; tis me rhysetai ek tou sōmatos tou thanatou toutou) — "Wretched man that I am! Who will rescue me from this body of death?" Talaipōros (wretched, exhausted from toil and conflict) — the most visceral emotional utterance in the letter. Tis me rhysetai (who will rescue me?) — the future tense: the rescue is still expected, not abandoned. Sōma tou thanatou (body of death) — the mortal body that is the site of sin's indwelling; rescue from this body = the bodily resurrection.
25: χάρις δὲ τῷ θεῷ διὰ Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ τοῦ κυρίου ἡμῶν (charis de tō theō dia Iēsou Christou tou kyriou hēmōn) — "thanks be to God through Jesus Christ our Lord!" The sudden doxological answer: the rescue of v24 is already known; the thanksgiving anticipates the full exposition of Romans 8. Ἄρα οὖν αὐτὸς ἐγὼ τῷ μὲν νοῒ δουλεύω νόμῳ θεοῦ τῇ δὲ σαρκὶ νόμῳ ἁμαρτίας — "so then, I myself serve God's law with my mind but with my flesh I serve the law of sin." The summary restates the divided condition; the Romans 7 egō remains divided until the Spirit's liberation in Romans 8. Paul does not resolve the tension in Romans 7 but in Romans 8 — the dramatic movement of the letter.
